topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of turpentine usually brought a strange, almost comforting clarity to my studio, but today, a sharp, metallic tang cut through it, making my stomach clench. I stared at the canvas—my latest piece, a swirling depiction of a stormy sea—and then I saw it. A stark, black line, precisely rendered, tracing the outline of a wave, an unmistakable tattoo. My breath hitched, a shallow, ragged sound in the quiet room. I slowly reached out, my fingertips brushing the ink. The texture was wrong, alien against the smooth oil paint. A wave of coldness washed over me, tightening my chest until I had to consciously draw a deep, shuddering breath. "What in the world?" I whispered, the sound brittle. My hands immediately recoiled, pushing the canvas back as if it were suddenly contaminated.

[story 2]

It was the sheer audacity of it that made my skin crawl. I had spent weeks perfecting the chiaroscuro in this portrait, every shadow meticulously placed. But underneath the rendered flesh of the subject’s collarbone, there was a pattern—intricate, dark, undeniably inked. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. I leaned closer, squinting, trying to rationalize the anomaly. It wasn't accidental; it was deliberate. A profound wave of revulsion rose in my throat, thick and bitter. I snatched up a rag, scrubbing furiously at the area, the friction doing nothing to erase the visual intrusion. "This is an invasion," I muttered to the empty room, my voice tight with barely suppressed fury. I backed away until the edge of my easel pressed against my spine, feeling a deep, unsettling churn within me.

[story 3]

My eyes tracked from the vibrant blues of the sky in my landscape to the figure sketched in the foreground. There, on the curve of the subject’s shoulder, was a complex arrangement of symbols, inked permanently into the skin. The color of the ink seemed to absorb the light, making the area look unnervingly flat. A tremor ran through my hand as I gripped my charcoal stick so hard the wood threatened to splinter. I stood motionless, my gaze locked on the mark. A feeling of profound wrongness settled over me, heavy and suffocating, pressing down on my lungs. I dropped the charcoal with a clatter that echoed too loudly. "Unacceptable," I managed, the word tasting like ash. I started pacing the floor, a restless, jerky motion, unable to look at the piece without feeling that sharp, unpleasant prickle across my skin.
